BELLESME. See BELLEME.
BELLEUSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Conty. Pop. 1,024.

BELLEVAUX, a village of Savoy, 6 m. SE of Chatclard, and 17 m. of Chamberry. It formerly contained an abbey. On the adjacent banks of the Cheran are some iron-works.—Also a v. in the district and 11 m. SSE of Thonon, on the l. bank of the Belleron. It has one annual fair. Pop. 1,080.
BELLEVEVRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Pierre, on the 1. bank of the Seille, 24 m. ENE of Chalons. Pop. 568.
BELLEVIEW, a village of Illinois, U. S., in Calhoun co., in a beautiful prairie, 85 m. SW of Springfield. Pop. 178.—Also a township of Missouri, U. S., in Washington co. Pop. 1,227.

BELLEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Pont-à-Mousson. Pop. 493.—Also a commune in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Charny, which affords good red wines. Pop. 603.— Also a commune and town in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Pantin, on the ENE of Paris, of which it may be regarded as a suburb. Pop. in 1831, 8,179; in 1841, 19,515. It is situated on a height, and is surrounded by numerous country-houses and nursery-gardens. It contains manufactories of cashmere, hair-cloth, fine leather, soap, cutlery, pencils, and paper, chemical works, and brass and steel-wire drawing-mills. In the environs are extensive gypsum quarries. B. was the scene of a conflict with the allied forces on the 30th March, 1814.—Also a commune in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Le Poire-sous-Bourbon. Pop. 333.

BELLEVILLE, a village of New York, U. S., in Jefferson co. Pop. in 1840, 300.—Also a village of New Jersey, U. S., in Essex co., 32 m. NE of Newark. It is beautifully situated on the W side of Passaic river, and comprises in its extent of 3 m. several fine mill-streams, and numerous manufactories. Pop. in 1840, 2,466.—Also a village of Illinois, U. S., cap. of St. Clair co., 13 m. SE of St. Louis. It stands high, is neatly built, and contains numerous manufactories. Pop. in 1840, 700.—Also a village of Arkansas, U. S., cap. of Desha co., on the S side of the Arkansas.—Also a town of Lower Canada, in the SW of the township of Thurlow, on the bay of Quinté, 50 m. W of Kingston. Pop. 2,040. It exports potash largely.

BELLEVILLE-SUR-SAONE, a canton commune and town of France, in the dep. of Rhone, arrond. of Villefranche. - The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 13.948.—The town is 9 m. NNE of Villefranche, and contains manufactories of embroidered calicoes, muslins, linen, &c. It has a considerable commerce in red wines, of which the environs afford some kinds of good quality. Pop. 2,436.

BELLEVUE, a fief of Lower Canada, in Vercheres co., between the seigniories of Vercheres and Contrecœur. It is 4½ m. in breadth, and 3 m. in depth, and although destitute of streams is generally well cultivated.—Also a township of Michigan, U. S., in Eaton co., 119 m. NNW of Detroit. The village is situated on Battle-Creek, and exports lime—the produce of the locality-in large quantities. Pop. in 1840, 529.—Also a v. in Jackson co., in Iowa, 22 m. above Charleston, on a fine plateau. The line of the great lead deposit commences here, and passes NW through the country of Dubuque and part of Clayton, and thence E to Wisconsin.
BELLEVUE-LE-TAMBOUR, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Aubenton, and com. of Any-Martin-Rieux. Pop. 82. BELLEVUE - LES - BAINS. See BOURBONLANCY. BEL
BELLEWSTOWN, a village in the p. of Duleek, co. Meath, 1 m. SE of Duleek. Pop. 77.

BELLEY, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Ain.-The arrond. comprises a superficial area of 135,391 hect., and contains 9 com., viz., Belley, Amberieux, Champagne, Hauteville, Lhuis, Lagnieu, St. Rambert, Seyssel, and Virieux-le-Grand. Pop. in 1831, 79,724; in 1836, 77,366; in 1846, 83,044.—The cant. comprises 23 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 16,405.—The town is situated in a fertile tract between two hills, about 5 m. from the Rhone, 29 m. SSE of Bourg, and 32 m. E of Lyons. Pop. in 1846, 4,474. It is the see of a bishop, suffragan of the diocese of Besançon; and contains a cathedral, an episcopal palace, an ecclesiastical school, a public library, a museum, a customhouse, &c. It possesses several silk manufactories, and silk-worms are extensively reared in the environs. Lithographic-stone abounds in the vicinity. A cattle and general fair is held once a-year.—This town—the ancient Belica—is of great antiquity, and was of considerable strength and importance during the Roman supremacy. It was destroyed by Alaric in 390, rebuilt in 412, and subsequently constituted an episcopal see. The present town owes its existence to Amedee VIII., duke of Savoy; and was ceded to France by his successor Charles Emmanuel, in 1601.
BELLHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Psalz, 6 m. from Gemersheim, on the Spiegelbach. Pop. 2,123.
BELLIAH, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 40 m. ENE of Bahar, 5 m. from the N bank of the Ganges.
BELLICAN, or BIAUT, a ruined city of Persia, in the district of Karabag, in the prov. of Azerdbijan. It was rebuilt by Timur, but has been abandoned for the new city of Shishe.
BELLICOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Le Catelet. Pop. 1,119.
BELLIE, a parish, partly in Morayshire, partly in Banffshire, on the S side of the Moray frith. Pop. in 1831, 2,432; in 1841, 2,434.
BELLIGAM, a village of the island of Ceylon, situated in a small bay on the S coast, 11 m. W of Matura. In the vicinity is a celebrated pagoda.
BELLINA, a village in the p. of Moyarta, co. Clare. Pop. 213.
BELLINA, or ABILIN, a village of Syria, in a beautiful valley of the same name, leading from the plain of Acre to the rich and fertile plain of Zebulon.
BELLINGE, a town of Denmark, in the island of Fyen, 5 m. SW of Odensee, and on the l. bank of the river Odensee.
BELLINGEN, a town of Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, in the bailiwick of Mullheim. Pop. 647.

BELLINGER, a river of New South Wales, of which the N arm rises in the New England plains, and flows SW till within 30 m. of the sea coast, whence, passing through the mountains, it pursues a NW course to the sea, and within 4 m. of its mouth is joined by the S arm, which, at a point 30 m. above this confluence, is an inconsiderable stream. The two arms by their junction form a basin of 7 m. in extent, in which at neap-tides a considerable number of sand banks make their appearance above water, and which discharges itself by a narrow channel over a sandy bar, 30 m. to the N of the Macleay, and 50 to the S of the Clarence river. Sydney is chiefly supplied with cedar wood from the low lands on the margin of the North B., being narrow belts of fine alluvial soil, thickly timbered, and extending from \( \frac{1}{4} \) m. to 1 m. from the river.

BELLINGHAM, a moorland parish in Northumberland, containing the market-town of B., and the townships of E and W Charlton, Leemailing, the Nook, and Tarretburn. Area 15,540 acres. Pop. 1,730.—The town of B. is 14 m. NW of Hexham, on the N bank of the Tyne. Pop. 672.-Also a township of Massachusetts, U. S., in Norfolk co., 36 m. SW of Boston. The soil is sandy, and possesses little fertility. The town is watered by Charles river, which affords good water-power, and its manufactures are considerable. Pop. in 1840, 1,055.
BELLINGHAUSEN (CAPE), a promontory on the E coast of the N part of the island of Saghalien, in N lat. 50°, and E long. 144° 15'.

BELLINGHAUSEN ISLANDS, a small group of low coral islands in the Pacific, in S lat. 15° 48', W long. 154° 30'. They stretch about 3 m. from N to S, and are connected by reefs enclosing an inland sea. They are covered with a thick growth of dwarf shrubs.
BELLINGSWOLDE, a town of Holland, in the prov. and 26 m. ESE of Groningen, 6 m. ESE of Winschoten. About 2 m. to the N is the frontier fortress of Oude. Pop. 2,400.

BELLINZONE, or BELLINZONA, in German, BELLENZ, a city of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, the cap. of the circle of the same name, 12 m. N of Lugano, at an alt. of 126 ft. above the Lago Maggiore, 696 ft. above sea-level, in 46° 11' 20" N lat., and 8° 56' 7" of E long. Pop. in 1808, 1,261; in 1834, 1,520. It is built on two rocks which are separated by the Ticino, and is on the route from St. Gothard to Milan. On the E side are two strong forts, and another on the W. The walls descend from the forts to the banks of the river, so that the three gates of the city form the communications of Switzerland with Italy on the side of St. Gothard, and the town commands the various passes which debouche into the Val Levantine from the Forest cantons, by St. Gothard; from Dissentis, and the valley of the border Rhin, by the Lukmanier; and from the Val di Misocco by the pass of the Bernardin. The forts were built in the 15th cent. by the dukes of Milan. On the side of Molignasco is an embankment—the Tondo Ripario—constructed by the French in the reign of Francis I., which serves to prevent the inundations of the Ticino, the Musa, and Calanchetto. B. is a well-built place. It has a collegiate church decorated with beautiful pictures, and a large seminary. Its streets, however, are narrow. It carries on a considerable commerce, and serves as an entrepot for merchandise to and from Italy, by way of the Lukmanier, St. Gothard, and the Bernardin. A fair is held in autumn for cattle and horses.—B. is supposed to be the Bilitio of early geography. The Romans denominated the valley of B. the Campi Canini. The Germans, endeavouring to penetrate into Italy by B., were defeated here by Majoranus. In 580 A. D. there was a castle nearly on the site of B. For more than 200 years it belonged to the dukes of Milan; and then successively fell into the hands of the Swiss and of the French, and finally of the Swiss in 1515. The latter, or the three cantons of Schweiz, Uri, and Underwald, remained possessors of B. till 1798. It was formed with the other Italian bailiwicks into the canton of Ticino or Tessin, in 1801.—The district of B. contains 130 sq. m. of surface, and 7,970 inhabitants, and is divided into 3 circles. The mountains in this district are composed of gneiss, and covered with forests of chestnut-trees. It is here that the climate and cultivation of Italy commence. Oranges and lemons are here reared, and two harvests of maize are annually reaped.
BELLM, a village of Hanover, 4 m. ENE of Osnabruck. Pop. 150. BEL
BELLMARIN, a village of Turkey, in the pash. of Bagdad, on the Euphrates, NW of Bassora.
BELLMONT, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, 2 m. E of Lake and 3 m. S of the town of Bunne.
BELLOA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Aragon, and 27 m. SSE of Calatayud, on the SW bank of Lake Gallocanta.
BELLO BERG, a mountain on the N confines of Venetian Lombardy, extending WSW from the Carnic chain of the Alps, in N lat. 46° 37', and E long. 12° 25'.
BELLO MONTE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, 2 m. to the S of the entrance of the Trigno into the Adriatic, and 12 m. WNW of Termoli.
BELLOCH. See BENLLACH.
BELLOCHIO, a town of the States - of - the- Church, 7 m. ESE of the town of Comacchio, and 13 m. N of Ravenna.

BELLOCQ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, and cant. of Salies. Pop. 1,145. BELLŒIL, a seigniory of Lower Canada, on the NW of the Richelieu river, and watered by a small stream of the same name which falls into the Richelieu opposite the Isles au Cerf. Pop. in 1836, 1,788.
BELLONA, one of the Solomon group, in the S Pacific, to the SW of Christoval. It is about 6 m. in circumf.
BELLONA ARSENAL, a village in Chesterfield co., Virginia, U. S., on James river, 12 m. above Richmond.
BELLO PORTO. See Porto Bello.
BELLOSGUARDO, a town of Naples, in Principato-Citra, in the district of Campagna. Pop. 1,395.
BELLOU-EN-HOULME, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Messei. Pop. 2,868.
BELLOU-SUR-HUINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Remalard. Pop. 937.
BELLOVAR, a town of Croatia, in the Warasdiner generalat. Pop. 1,054.

BELLOWS' FALLS, a village in the state of Vermont, U. S., in Windham co., 98 m. SE of Montpelier, romantically situated on the falls of the Connecticut. This river, which above the village is 350 ft. broad, here descends through a channel only 16 ft. in width. It is crossed by a bridge 365 ft. in length, supported in the centre by a natural pier of rock. The navigation of the river is maintained by a canal 3 m. in length.

BELLPUIG, or BELPUIG, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 17 m. E of Catalonia, and 46 m. NW of Tarragona. It is generally ill-built; but in its environs is a fine Franciscan convent, of Gothic architecture, and partially built of white marble. In the church is the magnificent mausoleum of the founder, Don Raimond de Cardona. Pop. 1,200.
BELLSHILL, a village in the p. of Bothwell, Lanarkshire, 3 m. NNE of Hamilton. Pop. 1,013.
BELLULCA, a Maronite village of Syria, in the pash. of Tripoli, 4 hours from Sholfatia.

BELLUNO, a delegation or administrative division of the Lombardo-Venetian kingdom, so called from its cap. It is bounded on the W and N by the Tyrol; on the E by the prov. of Udina; on the SE by that of Treviso; and on the SW by that of Vicenza. Its length is upwards of 50 m.; its breadth 18 m.; and its superficies 1,250 sq. m. The climate is mild, pure, and healthy. This district, comprised between the chain of the Alps and two principal branches of these mountains, is contained in the upper basin of the Piave. With the exception of this river, only a few torrents and small lakes occur within the district: amongst these is that of the Alleghe, which is supposed to owe its existence to the sudden sinking of a mountain. The hills and valleys produce grain, flax, vines, and other fruits. The extensive and rich meadows favour the rearing of cattle. The forests are considerable; those of Conseglio and Cajado furnish masts and excellent wood for building. The wood is transported in floats on the Piave to the lagunes of Venice. The mountains contain marble, iron, lead, copper, and vitriol. Silk-worms are reared in the environs of Feltri. The prov. of B. comprises 66 communes and 8 districts; and in 1837 had a pop. of 136,835. The districts are Agordo, Auronzo, Belluno, Feltri, Fonzaso, Longarone, Mel, and Pieve di Cadore. Belluno, the cap. of the above prov. and district, is situated on the r. bank of the Piave, at the confluence of the Ardo, 32 m. N of Treviso, and 50 m. N of Venice, in N lat. 46° 7′ 46", and 12° 13′ 36" E long. Pop. 9,731. It is well built, and surrounded with an old wall. It contains several churches, convents, and hospitals; and a large library, founded by the prelate Lollini. It has a commerce in wood with Venice; and possesses manufactories of silk, wax, leather, hats, and pottery, a weekly market, and several annual fairs. An aqueduct conveys water to the city from the distance of half-a-league.—Buonaparte conferred the title of duke of Belluno on Marshal Victor.

BELLUS, or Belusca, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 15 m. NE of Trentshin, on the l. bank of the Waag. It has a castle and a Catholic church, and possesses some pottery manufactories. In the environs are thermal sulphureous springs and baths. Pop. 1,859.
BELLVER, or Belver, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Catalonia, on the Segre, 12 m. WSW of Puicerda, and 15 m. ESE of Urgel.
BELLYE, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Baranya, 3 m. NE of Eszek, near the Moravitza. It has a castle, built by Prince Eugene of Savoy, and two churches. Pop. 2,800.
BELMONT, one of the Sidlaw hills, in the district of Strathmore in Perthshire. Alt. 759 ft.

BELMONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, and arrond. of St. Affrique. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 6,106. The town is on the r. bank of the Rance, 13 m. SSW of St. Affrique. Pop. 2,151. -Also a canton and commune in the dep. of Loire, and arrond. of Roanne. The cant. comprises 8 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 10,434. The village is 17 m. NE of Roanne, on the r. bank of the Sarnin. Pop. 3,184.—Also a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, in the dist. and 3 m. SW of Yverdun. In the vicinity are the ruins of an old castle.

BELMONT, a county in the state of Ohio, U. S. It contains a superficial area of 536 sq. m., bounded on the E by the river Ohio, and is watered by Indian, Wheeling, and Captina creeks. It is generally hilly; and its higher summits, especially towards the E, command prospects of great extent and beauty. The soil varies in fertility. Cap. Clairsville. Pop. in 1830, 28,412; in 1840, 30,901.—Also a township of Maine, in Waldo co., 37 m. NE of Augusta. Pop. 1,378.— Also a township of New York, in Franklin co., 10 m. E of Malone. It is covered with lofty hills, and is intersected by Chateaugay and Trout rivers. Its soil consists of clay and sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 472.
BELMONT-PRES-LALBENQUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot and cant. of Lalbenque. Pop. 1,573.

BELMONTE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, near the l. bank of the Tezere, 15 m. SSE of Guarda, and 34 m. NNE of Castello-Branco. Pop. 1,200.—Also a town of Spain, cap. of the judicial partido of the same name, in the prov. of Asturias.— BEL name, in New Castile, in the prov. and 45 m. SW of Cuenca, and 18 m. NW of St. Clemente. It has a castle of considerable strength. The environs are extremely fertile.—Also a town in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Madrid, near the N bank of the Tagus.- Also a town of Naples in the prov. of Sannio, in the cant. and 3 m. E of Agnone, and 20 m. NNW of Camporasso. It is situated on a rocky hill, and contains 3 churches and an hospital. The environs afford excellent wine. Pop. 1,200.-Also a town of Calabria Citra, in the dist. and 16 m. S of Paola, 2 m. N of Amantea, and 16 SW of Cosenza. It stands on a mountain acclivity, near the shore of the Mediterranean, and contains a strong castle and 4 churches. Silk worms are extensively reared in the environs. Pop. 2,914.

BELMONTE, a district and town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, on the r. bank of the Rio Grande e Belmonte, or Jequitinhonha, 30 m. N of Porto-Seguro, in S lat. 15° 51′ 4". Pop. 600.—The district is bounded on the N by the river Commandatuba; on the E by the sea; on the S by the river Mugiquicaba; and on the W by the Cordillera des Aimores. Pop. 1,200.

BELMONTE (RIO GRANDE E), a river of Brazil, formed by the junction of the Jequitinhonha or Diamond, and Arassuahy rivers, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, and comarca of Serro Frio. From this point of union it bends in an ENE direction, and enters the Atlantic at the town from which it takes its name, in S lat. 15° 50'. Its principal affluent is the Piauhy. In its passage through the Cordillera des Aimores, in W long. 40°, it contracts in breadth, and descends in a cascade, the noise of which is heard at the distance of 12 m. It forms, near its embouchure, several inosculations with the Patype.
BELMORE, a mountain on the N edge of the lower Lough Macnean, in co. Fermanagh. Alt. 1,312 ft.
BELMULLET, a town in the p. of Kilcommon, co. Mayo, on the isthmus between Broadhaven and Blacksod bay, 32 m. WNW of Ballina. Pop. 637.
BELNA. See BEAUNE. BELŒIL, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 17 m. ESE of Tournay. It has a fine castle, belonging to the princes of Ligne, and possesses excellent breweries.
BELOGOLOVSKA, a town of Kamtschatka, at the entrance of the river of the same name into the sea of Okotsk.
BELOI. See BIELOI. BELO PULO, PARAPOLI, or KAYMEN, a small island of the Grecian Archipelago, in the gulf of Argos, sanjak of Naxia, in N lat. 36° 57' and E long. 23° 26'.
BELO OZERO. See BIELO OZERO.
BELORADO, a town of Spain in Old Castile, cap. of the partido of the same name, in the prov. and 27 m. E of Burgos, on the Tiron.
BELOTIEH, a town of Turkey, in Servia, in the sanjak and SW of Semendria.
BELOVAR. See BELLOVAR.

BELP, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. and 6 m. SE of Bern, cap. of the bailiwick of Seftigen, at the base of the Belpberg, near the confluence of the Gurben and Aar. It suffers frequently from the inundations of the latter. Pop. 3,146. The Belpberg, which runs parallel to the Aar, rises to the height of 2,970 ft. above sea-level, and commands a magnificent view of the Alps. It contains mill-stone, and finely veined grey and white marble. On its eastern declivity are extensive fossil formations.
BELPASSO, a town of Sicily, in the dist. and 9 m. NW of Catania. Pop. 5,000. To the E of this town are the ruins of Hybla Major.

BELPECH, a canton, commune, and town of Also a town, cap. of the judicial partido of the same | France, in the dep. of Aude, arrond. of Castelnaudary. The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained | in 1831 a pop. of 6,101. The town is situated at the confluence of the Lers and Vixiege, 15 m. SW of Castelnaudary, and contains an hospital. It has some cloth manufactories, and 6 annual fairs. It was taken by the English in 1369. Pop. 2,452.

BELPER, a market-town and chapelry in the p. of Duffield, county of Derby; 134 m. NNW of London, and 7 m. N of Derby; intersected by the North Midland counties railroad. The town stands in an agreeable situation on the Derwent, over which there is a handsome stone-bridge of three arches. Till 1777, B. was an inconsiderable village, inhabited principally by nailers. The manufacture of nails is still extensively carried on here; but the town has risen into importance chiefly since the establishment of the cotton-mills of Messrs. Strutt. The weaving of linen-yarn into sheeting, checks, &c., is also carried on to some extent here. B. is a polling-place for the county. Pop. in 1801, 4,500; in 1831, 7,890; in 1841, 9,885.
BELPRE, a township of Ohio, U.S., in Washington co., 116 m. SE of Columbus. It stretches along the river Ohio, and consists, to a great extent, of meadow land.
BELSAY, a township in the p. of Bolam, Northumberland, 15 m. NW of Newcastle. Pop. 312.
BELSIA. See BEAUCE.
BELSITO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, within the jurisdiction of Cosenza, on the road from Rogliano to Cosenza.
BELSTEAD, a parish in Suffolk, 3½ m. SW of Ipswich. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 261.
BELSTONE, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. SE of Oakhampton. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 208.

BELT (GREAT and LITTLE), two narrow straits which connect the Baltic with the Cattegat. The former lies between the islands of Zealand and Funen, and varies in depth from 5 to 20 fath. Its shores are seldom bold or lofty, and they afford convenient harbours and anchorage. Its length is about 70 m.; its greatest width is about 20. It is divided into two branches by the island of Langeland, one of which is only 4 m. in width; the other is 8 m. The chief danger in the navigation of this strait arises from the sand-banks, and the number of small islands. Passage-boats cross in summer from Nyborg in Funen, to Corsoer in Zealand, a distance of 18 m., in three or four hours; and a speedier communication is now made by steam-boats. In the middle of the passage lies the small island of Sprogöe. Vessels passing this strait pay toll at Nyborg, where a guard-ship is stationed to enforce compliance.—The LITTLE BELT separates the low sandy island of Funen from the coast of Jutland. It is 80 m. in length; and, at the fortress of Fredericia, where the tolls are levied, does not exceed 1 m. in width, so that the entrance from the Cattegat is completely commanded at this point. In other parts, however, the strait expands in width to 8 or 10 m. The passage is usually made from Arroesund on the main, to Assens on the isle of Funen; but the route from Jutland to Copenhagen, by way of Fredericia, though circuitous, is preferred on account of the regularity and security of the conveyance. The shores of the Little Belt are seldom steep or rugged, but present several sandbanks; and the current from the Baltic to the Cattegat is of great strength. The depth varies from 4 to 27 fath.—The passage of both Belts is attended with considerable risk for large vessels, which on that account generally pass through the Sound; the Great Belt, however, was much frequented by British ships, and even by men-of-war, during the stoppage of the Sound, from 1807 to 1814, in consequence of our hostilities with Denmark. BEL
BELTA, a river of the Sahara, in the dist. of Sahel, which falls into the Atlantic ocean, in N lat. 26° 40′.
BELTCHOV, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Bulgaria, in the sanjak and 22 m. WSW of Rustchuk, near the r. bank of the Jantra, and 2 m. above the confluence of that river with the Danube.

BELTEK, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 15 m. S of Szathmar, situated in a plain, watered by a brook which falls into the Kraszna, 8 m. SE of Kiraly Darocz, and 56 m. E of Debrecin. Its environs afford good wine.

BELTIRIS, a tribe of Toorkish blood who inhabit the SE confines of the gov. of Tomsk, on the banks of the Abakan, in the SW of the gov. of Yenisei, in Russian Asia. They bear considerable resemblance to the Saghaians; but are distinguished by the practice of depositing their dead in trees, with the clothes and implements belonging to the deceased, and a small quantity of provisions and salt: a practice followed by some New Zealand tribes. The number of taxable individuals belonging to this tribe does not exceed 150.

BELTON, a parish in Leicestershire, 7 m. ENE of Ashby-de-la-Zouch. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 718. -Also a p. in Lincolnshire, 3 m. NE of Grantham. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. 176.—Also a p. in Lincolnshire, 11 m. NW of Gainsborough. Area 8,530 acres. Pop. 1,706.—Also a p. in Rutlandshire, 4 m. WNW of Uppingham. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. 402.-Also a p. in Suffolk, 4 m. SW of Yarmouth. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 465.
BELTRA, a lake in co. Mayo, on the SE side of Croghmoyle mountain, [alt. 1,412 ft.] Area 438 acres.

BELTURBET, a town in the parishes of Drumlane and Annagh, in co. Cavan, on the river Erne which flows through it, 7 m. NNW of Cavan. Pop. 2,070. The Erne, when its waters are high, is navigable to this town for barges.
BELUCHISTAN.

BELUJISTAN, or BELOOCHISTAUN, a country of Southern Asia, within the general confines of India and Persia, and formerly subject to the latter, corresponding to the Gedrosia of the ancients. The name Beluchistan, signifying 'the Country of the Beluchis,' is comparatively modern, and was first applied to the mountainous country S of Afghanistan, but has of late years been extended to the whole tract westwards to Kerman and Laristan, N to Sistan, and S to the sea, including the whole of Kohistan and Mekran. Taken in this extensive sense, B. is bounded on the N by Sistan and Afghanistan; on the E by Sinde; on the S by the Indian ocean; and on the W by the Persian districts of Kerman and Laristan. Sinde might, perhaps, be viewed as belonging to it, as a great part of its pop. are Beluchis. The maritime coast extends from Cape Jask, in 57° E long., to Cape Monze in 66° 58' E long., or nearly 600 m. Its extreme breadth is from the parallel of 24° 55' to 30° 40' N lat.; but its general breadth is from 25° to 30° N lat., or nearly 350 m. The area, according to Balbi, is 146,000 sq. m.; other calculations carry it to 160,000 sq. m. It probably exceeds even this latter estimate by 30,000 or 40,000 sq. m. Divisions.] B. taken in its largest acceptation, includes: 1st, B. Proper, comprehending the districts of Shaul and Mustung, Sarawan, Kelat, and Jhalawan; 2d, Lus; 3d, Mekran; 4th, the Desert; 5th, Kohistan, or the Highlands W of the Desert; 6th, Cutch-Gundava, and the district of Hurrund Dajil. Physical features.] The physical features of this country differ considerably in each province; but its general distinguishing characteristics are its rugged and elevated surface, -its extreme barrenness, -and its deficiency of water. Cutch-Gundava and Hurrund Dajil, to the E of B. Proper, are flat; whilst the latter district is a confused mass of mountains and elevated arid plains. Lus is a valley enclosed on three sides by the Brahuick mountains, and watered by the Puralli river. Maritime Mekran is a dry, barren, sandy tract, extending between the sea and the mountains, in some places receding 100 m. inland, in other places approaching within 25 m. of the sea. Interior Mekran is composed of deserts and mountains, ramifying in all directions, whilst the streams which descend to the sea are mere torrents, deep and rapid in winter, and dry in summer. Mountains.] The Brahuick mountains, or Hala range, are the most important physical feature in this country, whether politically or geographically considered. In both these respects they have always constituted the natural and political boundary of Persia and India, and the western limit of the basin of the Indus. Springing up abruptly from the sea, in the two conspicuous headlands of Capes Urbu and Monze, they run NE and NW in two arms, and then meet in one mass to the N of the Kohunwat, thus encircling the district of Lus like a crescent, of which the sea is the base. The combined mass runs almost due N to 30° N lat., where it meets the mountains of Afghanistan, after being cut across by the Bolan pass. Beyond this point the range decreases so much in magnitude and elevation that it sinks to a level with the hills of the Caukers, which form an intermediate lower level between the Brahuick mountains and those to the NE; but beyond this point it rises again to its former height. From Cape Urbu, on the SW point, to the NE of Nushki, the length of the Brahuick range is 425 m. SW and NE; but from the Kohunwat, on its southern verge, the length to Nushki due N, is 250 m., and the eastern side from Cape Monze extends 350 m. in a diagonal line. About 90 miles NE of Cape Monze, the Brahuick range sends off a branch as far as the Indus, which washes its base at the fort of Sehwaun. The breadth of the range, at its emergence from the sea at Cape Urbu, is not above 30 m. between the opposite sides of the base,—an extent not at all proportioned to its great altitude. At the Kohunwat the breadth does not exceed 40 m.; but it gradually increases, till in 28° N lat., it exceeds 200 m. from E to W, and in 30° N lat. 275 m. At 25½° N lat. the range runs progressively NNW and WNW, comprehending several degrees of long., and projecting many collateral ranges,—all inferior to the main one-some of which pass through the whole length of Beluchistan, and join the mountains of Persia; others elongate southerly till they touch the sea, or come within a few miles of it; whilst the western side of the main mass stretches away NW by N to 28° N lat., where it meets the SE termination of the Desert in 64° E long.; runs N and NE to Nushki, in 30° N lat.; and thence runs more easterly, till it joins the Afghan hills, and can no longer be traced as a distinct chain. Among these ridges, however, there are intermixed, in this quarter, numerous towering ridges coming from the NE and terminating on the SE edge of the Desert, between 64° and 65° E long. Within a few leagues of this spot, where the western face of the main range is repelled by the sandy desert, an immense mass disengages itself, and diverges in a variety of ramifications to the W and S. Of the former, one superior in bulk and elevation establishes a barrier to the encroachments of the sandy waste, by running along its S side for 2° direct W. It then suddenly runs 50 m. to the NW; and then gradually assumes a direct western course, converging finally with divers lesser ranges coming from the W, in 31° N lat. Thus united, they form a narrow, but, in some instances, a very low range, running, between 59° and 60° E long., so far N as to seem in some measure to join the Paropamisan range to the SW of Herat. This chain divides the desert of Kerman from Sistan, and marks the limits of Eastern and Western Persia. About a degree and a half to the S of the point where the range above-mentioned projects W from the main body, another range runs W from the main mass, and parallel with the former, for more than 230 m. Both of these ranges reciprocally protrude arms connecting them so intimately with each other, that, were it not for the intervention of some capacious and barren flats, added to their total eventual disjunction, they might have been taken as one mass. At the western termination of its course, it meets another great pile of mountains coming from the W, and diverges into two branches, running one to the N, and the other to the S, both of which, after making a short circuit, reunite and run to the SW, till it becomes identified with the mountains of Laristan. From its commencement to its final termination, many branches project to the S, and form headlands on the coast, as Cape Mobarek, and others on the confines of Persia. The ranges terminating on the W of Mekran coalesce in a very confused mass called the Kohistan, or 'the Highlands.' The most western district of the Kohistan is called Bushkurd,—a compound term, signifying 'the residence of the Kurd.' Some of its towering peaks are visible from Bunpur, at more than 100 m. of direct distance. Previous to the junction of all these ranges in the Kohistan, they send off numberless inferior rocky ridges which stretch, in uneven and oft-interrupted lines, across the NE of Mekran. These are slowly and imperceptibly compressed on one side by the wilds of Kerman, and on the other by that range which is turned to the N by the Kohistan mass, until they meet and conjoin in the manner above-mentioned. In a SW bend of these ridges, constituting the continuation of mountains which Pottinger passed the day he quitted Basman, the Kohi Nushader, or sal-ammoniac mountain, is found. All these end in abrupt cliffs on the edge of the sandy waste separating Bushkurd from Nurmanshir, and are excessively rugged from base to summit. The Kohistan may be regarded as a northern inland projection of Mekran, reaching to 30° N lat. This northern projection of the Kohistan is denominated in Persian Sirhud, or 'the Cold region,' and its peaks are seen soaring above all the rest to 90 m. distance N of Pottinger's route. From the Kohistan a branch runs NW along the southern frontier of the district of Nurmanshir, whilst another forms its northern boundary. Deserts.] There are two extensive deserts in this country, the one in the interior of Mekran, called the Desert of Bunpur; and the other to the N of Mekran, and W of Sarawan, denominated the Desert of Beluchistan. The sandy waste of Bunpur lies to the SW of the Kohistan, or highlands of Mekran, and SE of the district of Nurmanshir, the eastern projection of Kerman. It is of an oval figure, 155 m. long, by 80 m. in its greatest breadth. The desert of Beluchistan is far more extensive, being 300 m. long, by 200 m. of medial breadth, according to Pottinger; but if we extend it N beyond the Helmund river, which merely interrupts it, and connect it westward with the desert of Kerman, from which it is separated merely by a narrow range of hills, we have a dismal and desolate waste, stretching 600 m. E and W. and 500 m. N and S, and presenting a superficies of 280,000 sq. m. This is exclusive of the Great Salt Desert of Persia, and the desert of Karakum, N of Khorasan. Maritime Mekran is a desert, barren, sandy region, though by no means so bad as it is described by Arrian, for water may always be obtained near the shore by digging, and the heat is somewhat tempered by the vicinity of the ocean. The red sandy desert of Beluchistan, crossed by Pottinger, is much worse than the Sahara of North Africa. It is composed of a fine red moving sand, furrowed like the waves of the ocean in a storm, perpendicular on one side like so many brick walls, and sloped on the other, with hollows between. The difficulty of crossing these waves and hollows can only be appreciated by those who have been compelled to traverse them. Rivers.] In such a region as we have now described, rivers are scarce, small, and shallow. Among the lofty mountain-ranges rivers of some magnitude would naturally be expected, but none such occur. The Bolan and the Mula are mere mountain-torrents, hastening to lose themselves in the arid sands of Cutch-Gundava. During his whole route from Sumeani to Shiraz,—a distance of 1,500 m., 1,300 of which were on as direct a line as the paths would admit from E to W,-Pottinger says he did not meet with a running stream sufficiently deep to take a horse above the knee, till he met the Bundemir near Shiraz. What inland streams occur are all mere rivulets, which, when swollen with rain, become rapid and dangerous torrents, but soon run off; towards the coast they are somewhat larger and more persistent. Climate.] From the varied configuration of this extensive region, a correspondent variety of temperature may be expected. Accordingly, whilst an Alpine cold reigns amongst the mountains and elevated uplands, a tropical heat prevails in the plains and sandy deserts. In the plain of Kelat itself, the cold of winter is excessive. That city, and the neighbouring districts, though scarcely more than 51° removed from the summer solstice, are subjected to a most rigorous winter, and snow lies in the vales from the close of November till February; but the alt. of this district is at least 6,000 ft. above sea-level. Rice, and other vegetable productions which require warmth of climate, will not thrive here; and wheat and barley do not ripen so soon as in the British isles. Snow has been known to fall in the plain of Kelat 15 days successively in the month of March. On the other hand, the heat of the low plains, valleys, and deserts, is excessive, especially from the reflected heat of the burning sands. The people of Nushki are compelled, during summer, to emigrate to the mountains for cool air and water. In the district of Cutch-Gundava the heat is intolerable during summer, and the pestilential simoom frequently blows, and is very fatal to the natives. Productions.] Where rivers are few and small, and water scarce, vegetable productions cannot abound. In the whole of B. there is scarcely a forest-tree, at least nothing that can be called a wood or forest, though plenty of thickets or jungles are to be found in the bottoms of the valleys, or skirting the beds of periodical torrents. The few arborescent plants mentioned by Pottinger are: the oriental planetree, mimosas, tamarisks, oleanders, hedysaras, ficus, melias, besides the tamarind, walnut, mango, and other fruit-trees common to Persia, Afghanistan, and India. Dates abound in Maritime Mekran. The mountains are studded with bulbous plants like tulips, whose fragrance is perceptible to a great distance. Kusheput, or 'the Grass of the desert,' also abounds there, and is used as winter store for the cattle. It grows in bunches or tufts, with thick coarse stalks, bearing long and serrated leaves. The assafœtida plant abounds in the district of Nushki. Both Beluchis and Brahuis are excessively fond of this plant; and eat it stewed in rancid butter more offensive and nauseous than the plant itself. The only fertile districts in B. are the low level tracts of Cutch-Gundava and Hurrund Dajil, between the mountains and the Indus. The wild and domestic animals of B. are very nu- BELUCHISTAN. merous, both tropical and European. The horses are strong, well-boned, and large, but usually vicious. Those sent to India are chiefly reared to the S of Kelat, and in Cutch-Gundava. Camels and dromedaries abound, and are highly prized by the Beluchis, -the former for burden, and the latter for speed. The sheep are of the dumba or fat-tailed kind; and are very numerous. Goats and black cattle are also extensively reared, chiefly by the Brahuis, who reside in temporary huts erected on any spot which offers pasturage. Neither reptiles nor insects are peculiarly abundant. Almost every kind of mineral is said to occur in B., but we possess no particular information on this head. The mountains are generally composed of a hard black rock, a species of secondary limestone. The Bolan pass runs through recent formations. Rock salt is said to be common to the westward; and on the road from Cutch-Gundava to Kelat is a range of hills containing salt of a perfectly red colour and aperient quality. Sulphur and alum are also found in its vicinity, whilst grey and white marble occur to the W of Nushki. In the Kohistan are traces of volcanic action. Population.] The inhabitants of B. consist of Beluchis, Brahuis or Brahuicks, Dehwars, Mekranis, Kurds, Hindus, and a few Afghans, with a detestable branch of the Luris from Luristan in Persia-all distinct classes, having no common origin. Respecting the first class, it is impossible to say from what stock they are derived; but they are probably not the aboriginal natives. Pottinger thinks them of Turkoman descent, as they resemble that race in every thing but language. He supposes them to have belonged originally to the Seljukians who settled in Persia during the 11th cent., and who were expelled subsequently by the Kharazmian princes, but not till they had dwelt sufficiently long in the country to acquire the modern Persian language, which they still speak. Neither they nor the Brahuis have a written language, nor consequently any history; all their accounts of themselves being merely traditional. More than half the words of the Beluchi language are Persian, disguised by a peculiar pronunciation. But Ebn Hawkel-who seems to have written his geographical work about the year 920-affirms that the Afghan tribes of Cutch and Baluch had already taken possession of the mountains of Mekran; that they spoke a peculiar language; and that in their barbarous manners and predatory habits they resembled the tribes of the Arabian desert. If this be correct, the account is fatal to Pottinger's theory, as this fact took place more than a cent. before the Seljukian Turks crossed the Oxus, and three centuries before their supposed expulsion by the Kharazmian sultans. Ebn Hawkel was undoubtedly mistaken in calling them Afghan tribes; but this does not invalidate his statement that the Beluchis then possessed the mountains of Mekran; and he clearly distinguishes them from the nomadic Kurdish tribes who then wandered in the deserts of Fars and Kerman. It is well known that a tribe of Tartar invaders, denominated the Sacæ, conquered Drangiana, which from them was called Sacastana by Isidore of Charax. May not the Beluchis be the descendants of these invaders, who were driven from it, during the vigorous sway of the Sassanian princes, to the mountains which separate Beluchistan from Sistan? The one theory is as probable as the other; and there can be little doubt but they are of Turco-Tartarian origin, when we reflect how many tribes from the North have successively crossed the Oxus, and settled in Persia. The Brahuis are equally numerous with the Beluchis, and seem like them to be of Tartar descent. They are probably a more ancient race than the Beluchis, and are perhaps the descendants of the Indo- Scythians of the ancients. Their language is different from that of the Beluchis, and is called the Kurgali tongue. It in no respect resembles the Persian in sound; but contains a great many Hindu words, having a strong resemblance, as they strike the ear, to the Punjabi dialect.—The Dehwars are an agricultural race, speak pure Persian, and in all respects resemble the Tajiks of Afghanistan. Pottinger conceives them to be the descendants of the ancient Guebres or Magians.—The Mekranis are the inhabitants of Maritime Mekran, a small, hardy, brave race of men. Their women are very plain-looking, and almost all of them affected with weak eyes, probably owing to the fine particles of sand from the desert constantly floating in the air. Their language is also modern Persian.—The Hindus are found scattered here and there over the country in small bodies, as indeed over all the Eastern world, wherever money is to be made, unless we except Japan and China. They conduct the miserable traffic of the country, and act as money-changers and agents to the native princes. As the Beluchis are the ruling tribe, both they and the still more numerous Brahuis are exclusively denominated Beluchis. The Beluchis proper are divided into the three principal distinct tribes of Nharoes, Rinds, and Mugsis; and these again branch out into a great variety of subordinate tribes, -as Kumburanis, Mingulis, Zukris, Panderanis, Naharis, Imam-Hoseins, Bezunjas, and others, too numerous to mention. A branch of the Nharoes, called Rukhsani, inhabit the district of Nushki, the confines of Sistan, and the district of Bunpur in Northern Mekran; and have nearly exterminated all the jugdalls, or cultivators of the soil. They also dwell in Cutch-Gundava, whither they emigrated from Mekran. They are a tall, hardy race of men, handsome and active; and, without possessing great physical strength, are inured to every species of fatigue, and to every change of climate and season. They are the most ferocious and predatory of all the Beluchi tribes, defy all law, and are restrained by no feelings of humanity. The Brahuis possess all the better qualities of the Beluchis with few of their mischievous propensities. In appearance they seem of a different stock, having neither the tall figure, the long visage, nor raised features of the Beluchis, but short thick bones, round faces, and flat lineaments. They are a quiet and industrious race of men; and their fidelity is such that the Beluchi chiefs retain them as their most confidential servants. Whilst the Beluchis inhabit the plains, and subsist generally by rapine and plunder, the Brahuis, on the contrary, inhabit the mountains, and subsist by their flocks, which yield them cheese and clarified butter, and coarse blankets. They are equal to the Beluchis in personal bravery, and inurement to all the fatigues of life, and the changes of seasons and climate; but excel them in physical strength. They use the same dress, and the same weapons as the Beluchis; but their women are not, like those of the latter, secluded from the society of men. They are divided and subdivided into an infinity of tribes and small clans. It is impossible to state the aggregate numbers of all these tribes of Beluchis and Brahuis, together with the other classes of Jats, Hindus, Mekranis, Luris, and Afghans. Elphinston estimated the Beluchis, as far as his inquiries could go, at 1,000,000; but thought the statement was much too low. Balbi estimates it collectively at 2,000,000, which appears a more probable estimate than the former; and this is the utmost that can be said on a subject where nothing certain can be obtained. According to a muster-roll possessed by the khan of Kelat, the Beluchi army amounts to 250,000 men. But this number is a mere oriental hyperbole; and his present list, as BELUCHISTAN. Pottinger was informed, contains 120,000 men, which, in his opinion, is more than double the number he could even raise on a great emergency. The khan of Kelat, though exalted in political statements as the sole ruler of B., is merely the head despot of many despots over whom he has little efficient power. His actual authority only extends to the mountainous territory of B. Proper, and the level districts of Cutch-Gundava, and Hurrund Dajil; the Jam of Lus, the chiefs of Mekran, the khan of Bunpur, and the shah of Puhra, being independent of him. Balbi's statement, therefore, of 150,000 men as the force which the khan of Kelat can raise seems absurd. The whole revenue of this mighty political personage, amounted, in 1810, to 350,000 rupees, or £47,500, obtained from Hurrund Dajil, Cutch-Gundava, and the market-dues of Kelat. The taxes are chiefly levied on land, which, if watered by wells, pays 1-20th, if by rains, 1-16th, and if by natural springs still more. The Beluchis and Brahuis are all professionally Sunnite Moslems, and strenuous enemies of the Shiahs. The Hindu natives are mostly of the same faith. The Luris of Mekran, unless greatly belied by report, seem to be among the most detestable of the human race in principles and practice. Their belief is that man was born to live, die, and be forgotten; that during his existence, if he is happy, he has only to desire the continuance of life; but, if otherwise, he is at liberty to finish his being by suicide. The females live promiscuously with the males in an unbounded incestuous commerce. They are a Persian race, and speak a dialect of the Pehlevi or old Persian. History.] This extensive tract corresponds, we have said, to the Gedrosia of the ancients, which lay S of Arachosia and Drangiana, and comprehended all the country from Carmania to the Indus. The name Gedrosia does not appear to have been known till the expedition of Alexander the Great; for it is not mentioned by Herodotus in his account of the satrapies of Darius Hystaspes. Though not named, however, by that respectable historian, it is obvious that it formed the 17th satrapy of the Persian empire: for the Garicanii corresponded to the people of Poorah mentioned by Arrian, and the Ethiopians of Asia to the inhabitants of Mekran and Haur. Rennel is, however, doubtful of the geographical situation of the Parycanii. The marches of Alexander throw little or no light on the subject, as he never crossed the Beluchistan mountains, nor marched through the interior of the country, but merely skirted the coast,-which, till this time, had never been explored,—whilst the division under Craterus marched through Arachosia and Drangiana, or Candahar and Sistan. Alexander, it appears, had heard enough of the Beluchistan mountains and deserts to deter him from the attempt of traversing them on his route homewards. The interior of this region seems to have been as unknown, indeed, to the Greeks, as that of Africa is to the moderns. They had heard that there was such a country, and that it was mountainous and desert, and that its mountains were inhabited by a race somewhat resembling the Scythians in their habits and occupations; and hence they denominated the eastern part of it Indoscythia. Posterior to the Macedonian conquest, all the armies that have passed from India to Persia, or from the latter country to the former—except that of Nadir Shah, who in 1739 marched down the W side of the Indus. to the city of Tatta, on his return from Delhi-have avoided Beluchistan. The Arabian army sent by the Khalif Alwalid, in the 99th year of the Hegira, marched along the coast of Kerman and Mekran; and the succeeding invaders of the Ghaznevide and Gauride dynasties, entered India by the valley of the Cabul river: but all the intervening country was terra incognita to the invaders. Baber, himself, was never further S than Dera Ghazi Khan in 29° 50' N lat., and he seems to have been quite ignorant of the geography of Beluchistan. In fact until two British officers, Pottinger and Christie, traversed it in 1809, Beluchistan Proper. and the provinces of Mekran and Lus were wholly unknown to Europeans; and it is to them, in conjunction with Mr. Elphinston and Mr. Masson, that we are chiefly indebted for what geographical knowledge we possess, imperfect though it be, of Eastern Persia. The modern maps of this region, down to those of D'Anville and Rennel, are little better than the tables of Ptolemy, or the Arabian geographers. The fact is, all that appears in the maps of D'Anville and Rennel, and in the superficial account of this region given by Malte Brun, is almost wholly taken from the Arabian geographers. Pottinger's work was the first that gave us anything like an accurate account of B.; and was, therefore, a most valuable addition to modern geographical knowledge. No authentic accounts of the Beluchis reach above two centuries back, for though they are incidentally mentioned at the commencement of the 10th cent. by Ebn Hawkel, yet it is only as a roving savage tribe of depredators. Upwards of two centuries ago the city of Kelat, with the surrounding country, was possessed by Sewah-Rajah, a Hindu chief. The inhabitants of that city and district being greatly harassed by the depredations of the people inhabiting Cutch-Gundava—the Beluchis of that quarter -the rajah to protect his subjects, sent for Kumbar, a Beluchi chief, and took him into his service. But this chief managed to depose the rajah, and seize the government. He was succeeded by four khans successively; the last of whom, Nussur Khan, was appointed Khan of Kelat in room of his brother, Haji Khan. When Nadir Shah was employed in the siege of Candahar, he sent a large detachment against the Beluchis, reduced them to obedience, and raised Mohabbeet Khan to the throne of Kelat. On his death, Haji, the second brother, succeeded, but was deposed by Ahmed Shah, who raised the third brother, Nu sur, to that dignity. Nussur reigned, as a tributary to the Durani sovereigns, till the year 1795. For his services to Ahmed Shah at the battle of Meschid he was rewarded with the districts of Shawl and Mustung, Cutch-Gundava, and Hurrund Dajil. He was succeeded by his son, Mahmud Khan, a prince of very inferior talents to his father. His dominions have been mightily curtailed by the Ameers of Sinde, and the Beluchi chiefs of Mekran. Since 1810, B. has been involved in civil commotions to such a degree that there is scarcely the semblance of a regular government. In 1839, when the British army entered the Bolan pass, on their march to Afghanistan, the conduct of Mehreb Khan was such as to induce the commander-in-chief to detach General Willshire from the army of the Indus to capture Kelat, which was taken by storm, and Mehreb Khan himself killed in the assault. In the following year, however, the governor who had been appointed by the British was overpowered by the natives; but in 1841, after having reoccupied Kelat, Nasir Khan, the son of Mehreb Khan was recognised by the British, and left in possession of the government. Confusion still prevails in the country, and B. has almost no political existence, either as one sovereignty, or as a confederation of independent chiefs, for confederation is impossible in a region where every chief is a robber.

BELUL, or BEILUL (RAS), a headland on the coast of the Red sea, 14 m. N 47° W from Sunnahboar island. There is a bay on its W side, 7 m. in length from E to W, and 3 m. in depth, which affords good protection from S winds. On the E side of the bay, 2 m. in shore, is a small v.

BELUM, a village of Hanover, in the duchy of Bremen, in the bailiwick and 2 m. WNW of Neuhaus, and 3 m. SW of the junction of the Oste with the Elbe. It possesses a small port, and has extensive cattle fairs. Pop. 200.

BELUND, a river of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, which takes its rise to the E of Rewah, runs ENE along the northern base of the Kymoor hills, and curving N and W, unites with the Tonsa 25 m. SW of the confluence of that river with the Ganges.

BELUR-TAGH, also called BELUT TAGH, and by the Chinese Theoremseling, a very lofty range of snow-clad mountains connecting the Hindukush on the SW, with the Muz-Tagh on the NE, and forming part of the great buttress of Central Western Asia. It corresponds to the Imaus Versus ad Arcton of Ptolemy. It seems to commence in E long. 71°, and N lat. 34° 30'; and runs NNE till, in N lat. 37°, it diverges into two great branches, the one running NW to the NW point of the valley of the Upper Oxus, and the other, or main branch, to the sources of the Oxus and Kamnia rivers, in a NE direction, and meeting the great range of the Muz-Tagh running to the NW. Their junction forms a culminating point or knot, in which three great ranges meet: viz., the Muz-Tagh from the ESE; the Belur from the SW; and the Pamir from the NW. Beyond this point, the Belur or Thsoungling runs N, passing by the southern source of the Sihun or Jaxartes, till it meets the Alak-Tagh, where this latter joins the Ming-Bulak, or Arjun mountains, coming from the NW. This last range, also called the Kynder-Tau, forms the northern boundary of the upper course of the Sihun, separating it from the Ultra-Kirghize, whilst the Belur itself separates it on the E from the territory of Kashghar or Chinese Turkistan. The SW part of this range is denominated the Badakshan I. BEL range, as it separates that country on the E from the upper basin of the Kamnia; and in 37° N lat. its NW branch separates the valley of the Koksha on the SW, from that of the Upper Oxus on the NE. This great range, throughout its whole course, is covered with eternal snow. It is lofty, broken, and precipitous; and there seem to have been only two known passes across it for caravans and armies, both of which are gained by following the courses of the two great rivers,—the Oxus and the Jaxartes. The former, by way of Badakshan, is the road taken by Marco Polo; of the other, by Oosh, up the range to the southern source of the Sihun, an account has been given by Izzet Oollah, who describes it as laborious, difficult, and dangerous from snow and ice. Our knowledge of this great range is very limited, as no Europeans have ever seen it or crossed it, except Marco Polo, since the days of the caravans mentioned by Ptolemy. Belur, the Beloro of Marco Polo, is the name not only of the above chain, but also of an elevated region to the E or SE of the Pamir plain or tableland over which Marco Polo travelled 40 days, and which he describes as a cold mountainous region. It seems to lie to the E of Badakshan. Nasrodden el-Toosi places Belur 4 degrees E of that place. This region is also called Bolour, or Beloor, by Rabbi Abraham Peritsol in his Itinera Mundi, and seemingly corresponds to the Sirhud of Elphinston. According to Chinese accounts it lies to the SW of Yarkund, and E of Badakshan, and may perhaps answer to the Poloculh of Hallerstein's map. Marco Polo describes the inhabitants as a savage, ill-disposed race of idolaters, subsisting by the chase, and clothing themselves with the skins of their prey. It is clear that the district he describes lay between Badakshan and Kashghar; and it may therefore be an intermediate alpine region between the Belur-Tagh and the Muz- Tagh. The inhabitants, say the Chinese accounts, have large lips and an aquiline nose, and wear mustachios. They have no letters, and are totally ignorant of the language of the inhabitants of Chinese Turkistan. They dwell in villages, and build their houses of earth and stakes. Their dress and their bonnets resemble those of the people of Andijan (in Kokan); and those of the men differ little from those of the women. The country is full of sands, and the soil is dry and saline; the produce of the soil is scanty. Mulberry trees, whose fruit dried serves for food, are numerous; the inhabitants also subsist much on goats' milk, which they mix with mares' milk. Their prince bears the title of Bi. He takes three young females from families which have five or six daughters, and two from those which have four or five, and sells them in the neighbouring cities of Turkistan and Andijan. The Chinese further inform us that the region of Badakshan contains 100,000 families, and that of Belur 30,000; which calculating four to a family, gives a population of 520,000 for Badakshan, and Belur.
BELUR, or Bolor, the Poloculh of Hallerstein, a city of Chinese Turkistan in 37° N lat. and 72° 49' E long., on the l. bank of the Belur river, 54 m. NE of Badakshan.

BELUS, a river of Syria, in the pash. of Acre, rising in a lake about 6 m. from the coast, towards the SE of Acre, and flowing into the bay of Acre. Of the sand of this river, glass was first made according to Pliny [Hist. Nat. xxxvi. c. 26.]; and it continued so late as the middle of the 17th cent. to supply the glasshouses of Venice and of Genoa.
BELVAL, a village of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, and cant. of Buzancy, 16 m. NE of Vouziers. It has some iron forges.

BELVEDERE, a hamlet of Saxe-Weimar, in the 20 circle of Weimar-Jena, and near the town of Weimar. ! It contains a fine castle belonging to the grand duke of Weimar, built in 1724.—Also a town in the Statesof-the-Church, in the deleg. and 6 m. WNW of Poretta, and 36 m. SW of Bologna.-Also a town in the deleg. and 8 m. ESE of Logano, and 19 m. ESE of Bologna.—Also a town in the deleg. and 19 m. W of Ancona. It has 6 annual fairs.

BELVER, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Catalonia, on the Segre, 10 m. SW of Puicerda, and 15 m. ENE of Urgel.—Also a town of Leon, in the prov. and 12 m. N of Toro, near the r. bank of the Sequillo.—Also a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, on the Tagus, 12 m. E of Abrantes.
BELVERDE, a town of Sicily, in the intendancy and 5 m. NNE of Catania.

BELVES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Sarlat. - The canton comprises 15 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,898.—The town is situated on the Dordogne, 15 m. SW of Sarlat. Pop. 2,363. It contains manufactories of oil, paper, linen, tanned leather, lime, brick, and tile-kilns. It has several general fairs and markets which are well frequented.
BELVIANES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, and cant. of Quillan. It has iron and steel works. Pop. 307.

BELVIDERE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, in the dist. and 17 m. WNW of Paola, situated on a hill bathed by the Mediterranean, between the gulfs of Policastro and Santa Eufemia. It possesses a castle and several churches and convents; and is pre-eminent in beauty and magnificent scenery. It is celebrated for its wine and dried fruits.

BELVIDERE, a township of the state of Vermont, U. S., in Lamoille co., 47 m. NW of Montpelier, on the W side of the Green mountains. It presents an irregular surface, and is watered by branches of the Lamoille. Pop. in 1840, 207. - Also a village of New Jersey, U. S., cap. of Warren co., on the Delaware, 57 m. NNW of Trenton, and intersected by Pequest creek, which affords good water-power. The Delaware is here crossed by a handsome bridge.— Also a township in Illinois, in Boone co. Pop. in 1840, 698.
BELVIDERE SHOAL, a group of rocky islets in the Asiatic archipelago, at the northern entrance of the straits of Gaspar, in S lat. 2° 10', E long. 107° 5'.

BELVOIR, a liberty partly in Lincolnshire, partly in Leicestershire, 7 m. W by S of Grantham. Pop. in 1801, 80; in 1841, 109. The houses and pop. at B., with the exception of the inn, are all connected with the duke of Rutland's magnificent mansion of Belvoir castle.—Also a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Doubs, and cant. of Clerval. Pop. 526.

BELZ, a town of Gallicia, in the gov. of Lemberg, and in the circle and 22 m. N of Zolkien. It is situated on the N bank of the Zolobia, in a plain surrounded by forests. It is of considerable extent; and contains a castle built in the 11th century, and two handsome churches. The houses are chiefly constructed of wood. Potash forms the leading article of manufacture and export. - Also a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of St. Lorient. The cant. comprises 4 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 7,359. The vil. is 11 m. SE of St. Lorient. Pop. 1,390.

BELZIG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, cap. of the circle of Zauch-Belzig, in the regency and 25 m. SW of Potsdam. It contains 3 churches, some manufactories of cloth and coarse linen, a paper-mill, and some breweries. Pop. in 1837, 2,353.

BELZYCE, a town of Poland, in the circle and BEN 14 m. WSW of Lublin, on the r. bank of the Bystrzyka. It contains 3 churches, and about 150 houses. It was the scene of a victory gained by the Poles over the Russians in 1831. Pop. 1,530.
BEMBAS, a town of Guinea, in the kingdom and 120 m. ESE of the town of Benguela.

BEMBATOOKA, a town of the island of Madagascar, on a bay of the same name, on the NW coast. The bay forms the estuary of several rivers. It is 17 m. deep, and 3½ m. across at the entrance, and inside nearly 8 m. About midway the shores approach leaving a narrow channel.
BEMBIBRE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 E of Leon, on the r. bank of the Boeza. It has a manufactory of hardware. Pop. 1,600.
BEMBRIDGE, a chapelry in the p. of Brading, Isle of Wight, 3 m. ENE of Brading.
BEMERSLEY, a township in the p. of Nortonon-the-Moors, Staffordshire. Pop. 211.
BEMERTON, a chapelry in the p. of St. Peter Fugglestone, Wiltshire. Pop. 109.
BEMFICA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, in the comarca and 9 m. NW of Lisbon, near the great aqueduct of Agoas Livres. It is surrounded by numerous handsome villas. Pop. 3,873.
BEMINI ISLANDS, a group of small islands to the N of the great bank of Bahama, in N lat. 25° 45', W long. 79° 16'.
BEMINSTER. See BEAMINSTER.
BEMMEL, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, near the r. bank of the Waal, 4 m. NNE of Nemiguen. Pop. 2,750.
BEMONT, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Neufchatel, 13 m. W of Boudry.

BEMPOSTA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, in the comarca and 21 m. SW of Miranda, near the r. bank of the Duero. Pop. 600. -Also a town in the prov. of Beira, in the comarca and 24 m. ENE of Castello-Branco.
BEMPOSTA (PINHEIRO DE), a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, in the comarca and 15 m. NE of Aveiro.

BEMPTON, a parish in the E.R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. NNE of Bridlington. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 313. BEN, BEIN, BEINN, or BHEIN, a frequent prefix to the names of mountains in the Gaelic language. It radically signifies 'head' or 'summit,' and corresponds to the pen of the Welsh; but in Scottish topography it seems to be generally applied to a mountain of considerable magnitude. Probably we have the same root in the words Penine and Apennine.
